mainText:
**Ethiopia’s Red Sea Ambition**
Eritrea seceded from Ethiopia in 1993, and five years later, a war broke out between the two that resulted in up to 100,000 deaths. Fighting paused in 2000 when the two sides signed a peace treaty, but tensions remained high for years, even if the conflict was not technically active.

In 2018, however, Ethiopian Prime Minister Abiy Ahmed promised in his inaugural address that he would sign a more permanent peace deal. He did so within months, accepting the international arbitrators’ ruling on the disputed border. The next year, he collected the Nobel Peace Prize for it. Abiy stated in his Nobel Lecture, "Peace requires good faith to blossom into prosperity, security, and opportunity."

Since then, Ethiopia has modernized and liberalized economically, building infrastructure like railways and dams, and opening the banking and telecommunications industries to foreign investment. The reforms appear to be paying off, with International Monetary Fund managing director Kristalina Georgieva praising Ethiopia’s "impressive progress" on its economic agenda, including sustained GDP growth.

But on Monday, as Ethiopians began voting in a general election that the ruling party was all but certain to win, the prospect of Abiy holding on to power prompted predictions not of peace but of conflict. The question is not whether Abiy will remain in power, but what his win could set in motion: a deepening turn toward autocracy at home and a territorial push that could reignite conflict across the Horn of Africa.

Abiy came to power after years of protests against the marginalization of Ethiopia’s largest ethnic groups forced his predecessor, Hailemariam Desalegn, to resign. Upon entering office, Abiy quickly freed political prisoners, championed press freedoms, and promised to reform an economy stunted by state controls. The IMF projects that Ethiopia will be the fastest-growing economy in sub-Saharan Africa this year, with real GDP growth expected to hit 9.3 percent.

Ethiopia also floated its currency, the birr, in 2024 and launched the Ethiopian Securities Exchange for trading stocks, bonds, and other financial instruments in 2025. After hovering above 30 percent inflation in 2022 and 2023, it fell into single digits by late 2025. However, it has since ticked back up, reaching 11.7 percent in April—still far below its peak but the highest in six months as fuel and food prices climbed.

Despite these economic gains, they have not been evenly distributed throughout the country. Vanda Felbab-Brown, a senior fellow in Foreign Policy at the Brookings Institution, noted that Ethiopia’s GDP per capita has roughly doubled since 2018, but emphasized that this does not mean that every citizen can access that resource. Growth has been uneven, she said, "not just along ethnic lines, but also by distance from the capital and the level of persisting violence or its absence."

Ethiopia is a country deeply divided both ethnically and regionally, with the largest ethnic group, the Oromo, concentrated in the southern and central parts, and the second largest, the Amhara, located in the northern parts. The Tigrayans live in the northernmost region of Ethiopia, and various other ethnic groups make up the rest of the 135-million-person nation.

Abiy’s government quickly abandoned ethnic federalism—the principle that power should be apportioned based on ethnicity—and instead aimed to create a new Ethiopian identity that transcends ethnic identity. This approach has alienated many ethnic groups who feel locked out of power.

In 2020, war broke out between Ethiopian federal forces and Tigrayan People’s Liberation Front militants who felt politically marginalized by Abiy’s government. The conflict left an estimated 600,000 dead, and the United States criticized the government’s conduct of the war, finding that its forces, along with the TPLF, Eritrean troops, and Amhara militias, committed war crimes.

The government cut off phone and internet access and restricted journalists’ access to the region during the conflict. The Committee to Protect Journalists ranks Ethiopia among sub-Saharan Africa’s worst jailers of journalists, second only to Eritrea.

The hope Abiy inspired was quickly tarnished, as noted by Roland Marchal, a researcher at the Centre for International Studies and Research. "He was blinded by his hatred of the T
